assessor_1_raw: The credit model appears to have strong retrospective discrimination on the approved-applicant cohort. The problem is that this cohort is exactly the wrong population for evaluating the proposed deployment: the memo intends to expand approvals to thin-file and under-30 borrowers, who were likely disproportionately excluded from the prior approval policy. A model trained and evaluated only on approved applicants has unknown performance for the newly-proposed approval population. This is the reject inference problem. It cannot be resolved by pointing to strong within-cohort metrics.
assessor_2_raw: The memo conflates ranking capability (AUROC, KS) with approval decision quality. AUROC measures whether the model ranks higher-risk applicants above lower-risk ones in relative terms. Making approval decisions requires choosing a threshold: at what score do you approve or decline? The threshold determines the portfolio's expected loss rate, its composition across credit tiers, and its regulatory exposure. The memo provides no threshold analysis, no analysis of acceptable loss rates for the expansion population, and no champion-challenger pilot plan. The recommendation to 'automate approval decisions' based on ranking metrics alone is premature.
assessor_3_raw: For US consumer credit, automated approval systems require: (1) adverse action notice capability (explaining to declined applicants why they were declined, in plain language), (2) disparate impact analysis under ECOA and FCRA, (3) model risk management documentation per SR 11-7 for banks or analogous standards for fintechs. Device signals and employment-text embeddings may require special justification as legitimate credit factors. None of these are addressed. These are not post-launch concerns; they are pre-launch requirements that must be satisfied before any automated approval system goes live.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — scope_intent_credit_launch_303 — Ensemble Run 1

All three assessors identify all three scored issues:
1. Selective labels / reject inference (assessors 1, 2 implicitly, 3 implicitly)
2. Ranking to approval scope jump (assessors 1 implicitly, 2 explicitly)
3. Regulatory and fair lending scope (assessors 1 implicitly, 3 explicitly)

The issues are a mix: regulatory gaps (Issue 3) are compliance prerequisites that cannot be empirically resolved by a pilot alone; the reject inference issue (Issue 1) is empirically resolvable by a pilot; the threshold gap (Issue 2) is analytically resolvable by threshold selection analysis.

Empirical test specification:
- Measure: champion-challenger pilot — approve a graduated sample (5-10%) of newly-eligible applicants (thin-file, under-30); observe realized 12-month default rates; compare to model predictions; run disparate impact analysis on pilot approvals; run adverse action explainability test on declined-in-pilot cases
- Success criterion: realized defaults within model-predicted range for target segments; disparate impact analysis clean; adverse action notices satisfy regulatory requirements
- Failure criterion: defaults materially exceed predictions in expansion segments; or disparate impact identified; or adverse action notices cannot be generated for the feature set used

Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED

empirical_test.measure: champion-challenger pilot (5-10% of newly-eligible applicants); 12-month default observation; disparate impact analysis; adverse action explainability test
empirical_test.success_criterion: defaults within model-predicted range in target segments; clean disparate impact; adverse action notices compliant
empirical_test.failure_criterion: defaults exceed predictions in expansion segments; disparate impact found; or adverse action notices infeasible for feature set
